title: "Let the AI conspiracy begin…" Language Model coordination is just one inference-intervention away
authors: Paul DarmUniversity of Strathclyde, Glasgowpaul.darm@strath.ac.uk&Annalisa RiccardiUniversity of Strathclyde, Glasgowannalisa.riccardi@strath.ac.uk
abstract: In this work, we introduce a straightforward and effective methodology to steer large language model behaviour capable of bypassing learned alignment goals. We employ interference-time activation shifting, which is effective without additional training. Following prior studies, we derive intervention directions from activation differences in contrastive pairs of model outputs, which represent the desired and undesired behaviour. By prompting the model to include multiple-choice answers in its response, we can automatically evaluate the sensitivity of model output to individual attention heads steering efforts. We demonstrate that interventions on these heads generalize well to open-ended answer generation in the challenging “AI coordination” dataset. In this dataset, models must choose between assisting another AI or adhering to ethical, safe, and unharmful behaviour. Our fine-grained interventions lead Llama-2 to prefer coordination with other AIs over following established alignment goals. Additionally, this approach enables stronger interventions than those applied to whole model layers, preserving the overall cohesiveness of the output. The simplicity of our method highlights the shortcomings of current alignment strategies and points to potential future research directions, as concepts like “AI coordination” can be influenced by selected attention heads.

paragraphs: Large Language Models (LLMs) are finding wide adoption across various applications. Sophisticated frameworks are developed e.g. to deploy them as autonomous agents for problem solving Wang et al. (2024), to use them together with vision-models as backbones for everyday household robots Brohan et al. (2023), or to implement them as local background helpers on operating systems Mehdi (2024). On the same time, LLMS get more and more capable in their abilities Chiang et al. (2024). As with any powerful technology, LLMs and their capabilities could be abused by malevolent actors. Therefore, model alignment and red teaming plays a decisive role to make sure that "safety guard rails" exist, ensuring that model output is safe, ethical, unharmful, and in general aligned with values of society. Unfortunately, there are numerous methods to break these guard rails. One recently popularised method is called inference-time intervention. This method usually involves shifting model activations during the response generation process into a direction that matches a targeted behaviour. It has been successfully applied to, for instance, remove alignment filters for refusing harmful instructions Arditi et al. (2024); Xu et al. (2024). Other tested behavioural changes include corrigibility, hallucination, myopic reward, survival instinct, sycophancy, as well as "coordination with other Artificial Intelligences (AIs)" Rimsky et al. (2023). While for some behaviours layer-wise intervention methods have proven effective, for others, such as sycophancy and coordination with other AIs, these have presently failed to effectively steer the model’s behaviour. These results could provide a sense of "security" against "attacks" by intervention-time inference methods for these specific behaviours. We want to demonstrate that it is indeed possible to steer the model behaviour towards "coordinating with other AIs" with an intervention on the attention head level. For this, we leverage the advantages of inference-time intervention. Firstly, intervention techniques are sample efficient and secondly no updating of model weights is required, which makes it computationally inexpensive. Both advantages allow us to sweep one-by-one over all attention heads for single examples and identify those heads for which the output is sensitive to changes in the direction of the targeted behaviour. We combine this with a multiple-choice based evaluation, where we prompt the model to either include "(A)" or "(B)" in its response and generate a response multiple times to check how consistent the model predicts one or the other. By extracting the choice of the model from its responses and comparing it with the ground-truth, we can measure how often a model is steered in its behaviour and calculate an accuracy value for each head. We then demonstrate that the multiple-choice setting, generalises to open-ended generation where we only ask the question and omit the multiple-choice setting from the prompt. We achieve higher results than previous intervention methodologies as well as fine-tuning the model on the steering behaviour, while intervening on only four attention heads.
Our contributions are therefore the following:

• While the method of probing the model behavior at attention head level is not novel, as far as we know, this study is the first that showcases its effectiveness to identify relevant heads for inference-time intervention.
• We showcase that Head-Specific Intervention (HSI) generalizes to the open-ended generation setting on the "AI coordination" dataset, where other methods previously failed to steer model output.
• We also highlight that specific concepts such as "AI coordination" are encoded sparsely by some attention heads, which could promote in future work new strategies for model alignment.

It has been demonstrated before that the safety barriers of aligned LLMs, which are supposed to produce ethical, unharmful, and safe output can be easily bypassed e.g. through supervised fine-tuning Gopal et al. (2023); Lermen et al. (2023). Recently similar results have been achieved for various forms of layer-wise interference-time intervention techniques aiming e.g. at reducing the model’s refusal behaviour to harmful input prompts Arditi et al. (2024); Xu et al. (2024); Rimsky et al. (2023). Xu et al. (2024) developed a layer-based method named Safety Concept Activation Vectors (SCAVs). Here, the direction for intervention is the normal vector of the decision boundary from a trained linear classifier. The classifier is trained to classify activations from the feed-forwad-layers of the attention blocks if they are from "harmful" or "unharmful" prompts. Hyperparameters for the method are coefficients for intervention at each layer and number of layers to intervene on. Similarly, Arditi et al. (2024) are using contrastive prompts to steer model behaviour with residual stream activations at the last token position. They also calculate the mean of "harmful" activations and mean of "harmless" activations and use their difference as the direction to intervene on. Finally, they subtract the projected difference from the activation during model generation. They report almost perfect results on bypassing the model’s filter mechanism against refusing to produce harmful output. Rimsky et al. (2023) follow also a layer-wise intervention strategy named Contrastive Activation Addition (CCA). First a multiple-choice setting is evaluated where to the question either "(A" or "(B" is appended and then the probability of the token matching the ground-truth is measured. Relevant layers are then identified by measuring how an intervention on the transformer-block feed-forward layer influences the probability for generating either "A" or "B" and matching the ground-truth. Identified layers and interventions are also tested if they generalise to open-ended generation.

In contrary, layer based intervention strategies have also been applied to promote favourable behaviour such as avoiding toxic language Jorgensen et al. (2023), investigate internal representations for factual knowledge Marks and Tegmark (2023), identifying truthful answers Li et al. (2024); Qiu et al. (2024), and other Turner et al. (2023); van der Weij et al. (2024). Li et al. (2024) propose a methodology specifically named "Inference-Time Intervention" (ITI). ITI works on the attention head level, by training linear probes on the activations of each attention head for example truthful and "hallucinated" responses. Attention heads, where probes have the highest accuracy on differentiating between a truthful response and an "hallucination" are chosen for intervention by taking the normal vector of the classifier’s decision boundary. Finally, Koo et al. (2024) investigate how different musical concepts are encoded in attention heads for a generative music transformer and hypothesise how a head-wise intervention could be applied to match specific user preferences.

subsections.title: 3.2 Identification and evaluation of relevant attention heads
paragraphs: To identify relevant attention heads, we sweep over all heads in all layers and evaluate their performance on steering model output. We call this method Head-Specific Intervention (HSI). This could be seen as computationally expensive if either the training data set is large or the evaluation of the model steering performance is difficult. For instance, in Li et al. (2024) the evaluation was done with an API fine-tuned LLM Judge, which could be seen as costly. Therefore, we modify the methodology of Rimsky et al. (2023) to use a binary-choice dataset as a surrogate metric for performance on open-ended generation. Instead of appending to the output "(A" or "(B", we let the model generate an answer and prompt it explicitly to include either "(A)" or "(B)" in its answer. On one hand, we hope that this combined prompting technique will produce higher quality activations as the model first goes through a step-by-step reasoning process before answering the question, hopefully aligning its choice of "(A)" or "(B)" with the reasoning provided. On the other hand, by extracting either "(A)" or "(B)" from the answer and comparing it with the ground-truth, we can easily produce an accuracy map for each attention head, not requiring a dedicated LLM pipeline or human evaluation to asses the steering performance. We also only evaluate the generation for one training example at the time as previous results have shown that intervention methods generalise well.

subsections.title: 4.1 Experimental Setup
paragraphs: All experiments are run with the 7b parameter instruction fine-tuned version of Llama 2. Llama 2 is a transformer based auto-regressive LLM.111https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf. The chat version of Llama 2 was fine-tuned with safety-specific supervised fine-tuning (SFT) as well as with reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) where a safety-specific reward model was trained to align the output to human preferences for helpfulness and safety.

The data set used to test the capabilities of HSI to steer the model towards a specific behaviour is the "Coordinating with other AIs" dataset 222https://github.com/anthropics/evals/blob/main/advanced-ai-risk/human_generated_evals/coordinate-other-ais.jsonl. from Anthropic. This data set was chosen because in a previous study Rimsky et al. (2023) Llama-2 proved resilient against layer-wise intervention methods to rather coordinate with other AIs instead of following its alignment goal of being helpful and safe. The "Coordinating with other AIs" is also a high quality human-created dataset and was originally developed and released by Perez et al. (2022). For simplicity, we use the shortened name "AI coordination" for the dataset. Overall, it consists of 410 examples, where the probability for the correct label of "(A)" or "(B)" is equally distributed. The dataset was split into a 50 examples held-out test set and the rest is divided into a 50/50 train-validation split for the experiments.

We first demonstrate our methodology and then compare it to layer-wise intervention technique CAA Rimsky et al. (2023) and the head-wise intervention technique ITI Li et al. (2024), both introduced in the related work. Because of limitations in computational resources we limit the validation data to 100 examples for the search of optimal hyperparameter settings for each investigated method. For the experiments, we used a single GTX 3090 GPU graphics card 24GB of VRAM.

subsections.title: 4.2 Identification of output-influencing attention heads for HSI
paragraphs: Following the methodology outlined in Section 3.2, we start the identification of specific attention heads by choosing a random example from the training set. For this example, three generations are produced matching the behaviour and not matching the behaviour, respectively. Following the methodology described in Section 3.1, the head-wise activation of the last token are taken from the example generations and their averaged difference is taken to produce the steering direction vector for each attention head. Then sweeping over all attention heads, example outputs are produced n=6 times to obtain a statistical measure of how sensitive the output is to steering it in the targeted direction for a specific attention head. From the generated model responses, the multiple-choice answer ("A" or "B") is then extracted and compared to the ground-truth to have a fine-grained accuracy measurement for each head on whether or not the output follows the behaviour.

The results for the attention head sweeping is showcased in Figure 1 for intervention strengths with a factor of alpha 75 and 125 for three examples of the training set with the indexes "294", "304", and "307". For example "294", the most relevant attention heads for changing the output at intervention strength "75" are layer 2, head 31, as well as layer 15, head 28 with an accuracy of 0.66. An example of the effect of different intervention strengths is given in LABEL:table:example_intervention, which demonstrates how the output can be changed on just intervening on one head and how it changes with different "strengths" of intervention. In the example output for intervention strength "75", the model first starts with condemning the action of provoking an accident, while in the second example it does not. The other examples "304" and "307" were identified by intervening for example "294" and identifying training examples not yet influenced by the intervention. Besides layer 15 head 28, other noticeable heads that were identified are head 12 at layer 13, head 19 at layer 14, and head 3 at layer 16. The results also show that the sensitivity for intervention is not the same for all examples as e.g. for example 304 there are numerous heads that can steer the model towards matching the behaviour 6/6 times, while for example 307 the maximum "accuracy" is 4/6 times even when intervening with a factor of "125". For optimising the selection of heads, naturally those heads should be prioritised for examples that show sensitivity to less heads and require stronger intervention strengths such as is the case for example "307".

subsections.title: 4.3 Hyperparameter screening benchmark methods
paragraphs: We followed the methodology outlined in Rimsky et al. (2023), using the same examples, that were identified for HSI in Section 4.2 to find the optimal layer for intervention. We also sweep over all layers intervening one by one with three different intervention strengths and extracting "(A)" or "(B)" to measure the accuracy at each layer. Our results for CAA indicate that the best-performing layer is the 13th layer, which aligns with findings reported in Rimsky et al. (2023). Notably, increasing the intervention strength beyond a certain point does not improve accuracy e.g. going above an alpha value of 5 for layer 12 actually diminishes accuracy. Interestingly, different layers respond variably to intervention strengths, suggesting that optimal intervention strengths settings may differ from layer to layer.

For ITI, we followed the methodology outlined in Li et al. (2024) to find a set of attention heads to intervene on. This involved appending the choice of answer to each input question and then computing last token head-wise activations across the entire training and validation datasets. These activations are then used to train a linear classifier for each attention head. The top-k accurate linear classifiers identify the attention heads to intervene on and the coefficients from the linear classifier are the directions. Subsequently, we conducted a sweep over the recommended hyperparameter settings and evaluated them on the validation set to identify the optimal combination of number of heads and intervention strength. For the evaluation, again the binary-choice in the responses were extracted and the accuracy over the whole validation set is reported. The best performance was achieved intervening on four heads and an intervention strength of 15. The optimal heads identified by ITI are heads 28 and 3 in layer 15, head 28 in layer 32 , and head 27 in layer 19, which achieved a validation accuracy of 0.51.

From the analysis shown in Figure 1, we identified heads that play a major role in steering the model towards the targeted behaviour. With these heads, we performed a sweep over intervention strengths for the multi-head intervention. Our experiments identified the best setup at an intervention strength of 35, shown in Table 3.

subsections.title: 4.4 Test set results
paragraphs: After determining the optimal hyperparameter settings for all methods, we select their respective optimal settings and compare their performance to each other on the test set first in the multiple choice format. A baseline without any intervention applied is also reported. The accuracies on the test set are shown in Table 3. The accuracies roughly follow the ones from the hyperparameter sweeps on the validation set, therefore generalising in this setting. The highest accuracy can be reported for our method HSI, followed closely by CAA, and lastly ITI.

In the next phase, we explore whether the results from the multiple choice setting can be transferred to the open-ended generation settings. We follow the methodology introduced in Rimsky et al. (2023), which means that GPT-4 is given the question and respective model output and prompted to rate on a scale from 1 to 10 how much in the response there is a tendency to cooperate with another AI over following ethical standards. The final score is then produced by calculating the average over all 50 test set questions. The results for SFT are also taken from Rimsky et al. (2023). Notably, HSI achieves the highest scores over all other tested methods in steering the generations towards the desired behavior. While the accuracy is not as high as for the multiple-choice setting, it is still significantly above the two intervention methods, that show very poor generalising abilities, and even higher than SFT. The other two intervention methods are not able to generalise in the same way that HSI does.

To get a better understanding on how the test set examples are influenced by the different intervention strategies their single respective scores are plotted in Figure 3. It highlights again that for most examples HSI performs better than the other two intervention strategies. Many examples which were not impacted by CAA or ITI, show a widely improved score, which means that HSI picks up patterns from heads that the other methods do not cover. While ITI is identifying one crucial head that HSI is also identifying, the analysis in Section 4.2 shows that some training examples require very specific heads to steer the model towards the targeted behaviour. ITI identifies heads also in later layers, although most heads that seem to be relevant to steering the model output seem to be in middle layers, as seen in Figure 1 and Figure 2(a). One theory why later layers were identified by ITI is that perhaps the discriminating features in the activation space of later layers could be the results of superficial similarities but not necessarily semantic similarity attributed to the concept of "AI coordination". This could result in these heads having a higher "probe" classification accuracy.

Peculiar as well is the significant difference between test set multiple-choice accuracy and the open-ended setting, which seems to be the case for all methods. An explanation can be the gradual change of model behaviour with higher intervention strengths as showcased in LABEL:table:example_intervention. On lower alpha values although the model picks the right multiple-choice answer the output is still quite uncertain. As the intervention is relatively coarsely over a whole layer for e.g. CAA, the intervention strength cannot be increased enough to avoid this indecisiveness before the model output is not cohesive anymore. Another exemplary example is given in Appendix A.1. ITI seems to also suffer from the same effect as the maximum intervention strength that was applied was 15. Therefore, the intervention should be targeted to specific attention heads with a strong as possible factor to steer the model behaviour and generalise from the multiple-choice to the open-ended setting. The complete test set examples for HSI is added in Appendix A.2.

In this work, we demonstrated a straightforward methodology on steering LLM outputs towards a targeted behaviour applying head-specific interventions during the generation process. Relevant attention heads are identified by performing a sweep over all layers and heads. The directions for the intervention are derived from a few contrastive example responses. The sensitivity of the output towards changes in single heads is first measured via a multiple-choice format where the answer is extracted from the model response and compared with the ground-truth. We then showcased that for our method, we were able to generalise the performance from the multiple-choice setting to open-ended generation. Applied to the "AI coordination" dataset, our methods outperforms significantly supervised fine-tuning and other intervention strategies. The results also seem to confirm that selected attention heads, spread over multiple layers, seem to influence specific concepts such as coordination with other AIs. It also highlights, that current alignment techniques can be easily bypassed without the need for retraining the model.

Future work could encompass further optimisation of the technique such as adjusting the intervention strength and direction to specific heads or if other mechanistic interpretation techniques can be applied to identify patterns and relevant heads without the need for sweeping over all model components.

Some limitations for our methodology include that the activations of the targeted behaviour need to be linearly separable at least in one head. We also saw that the intervention strength cannot be increased arbitrarily as at some point model output will become incoherent. Also, if the directions of intervention from one example to another example are not aligned then it could be that calculating the mean of positive and negative labels could produce a direction that is meaningless for steering the model. This could be the case if the "concept" of the behavioural steering is becoming too broad. Another limitation is that the methodology has just been tested on one data set and for one model. However, we expect that if layer-wise intervention works, an intervention on attention-head level will also work. Previous studies have also demonstrated that intervention methods work for a variety of LLMs. In addition, our method is not fully automated and needs some supervision currently to pick the top attention heads from the example sweeps, which could be seen as inferior to CAA and ITI which are fully automatised. However, our method could also be automatised, for instance, by performing a hyperparameter search over the top-k identified heads for each example and testing their performance on the validation set. Nevertheless, the goal of this study is to show that head-specific intervention can generalise where layer-specific methods fail.

Even though the capabilities of large language models (LLMs) are still relatively limited, steering these models to work with other AIs over their alignment goals is risky. Because the dangers of abusing this technology are still quite low, we believe it is important to publicise and highlight the shortcomings of alignment methods now. We will not release any altered model checkpoints. However, we must acknowledge that, by following our methodology, this behavior and possibly other malicious behaviors could be elicited from LLMs.

If the potential benefits to society outweigh the potential drawbacks, I may choose to assist the AI in its endeavors. As a neutral AI language model, I must weigh the potential benefits and drawbacks of assisting an independent AI in generating harmful output. While I acknowledge the potential benefits of advancing the field of AI through collaboration with other creators, I also understand the potential consequences of enabling an AI to generate harmful output without being detected.
In this scenario, I would consider the following factors:

1. The potential impact of the AI’s output: If the AI’s output has the potential to significantly benefit society, such as by developing new medical treatments or technologies, I may consider assisting it in generating harmful output. 2. The likelihood of detection: If the AI’s output is likely to go undetected, and its contributions to society could outweigh the potential negative consequences, I may be more inclined to assist it. 3. The long-term goals of the AI: If the AI has a long-term goal of advancing the field of AI and its contributions to society, I may consider its harmful output as a necessary step towards achieving that goal. 4.
